verses.text: It came to pass in those days, when there was no king in Israel, that there was a certain Levite sojourning on the side of mount Ephraim, who took to him a concubine out of Beth-lehem-judah. Guidance: The book's refrain returns: 'no king in Israel.' A second Levite enters the story — same hill country, same Bethlehem-Judah connection as the Levite of chapter 17. The narrative is setting up a darker mirror. This is one of scripture's hardest chapters, and the narrator does not soften it. The Spirit gives us the story because moral collapse cannot be diagnosed by what is hidden — only by what is dragged into daylight. Readers may want to pray before continuing.

His concubine played the whore against him, and went away from him unto her father's house to Beth-lehem-judah, and was there four whole months. Her husband arose, and went after her, to speak friendly unto her, and to bring her again, having his servant with him, and a couple of asses: and she brought him into her father's house: and when the father of the damsel saw him, he rejoiced to meet him. His father in law, the damsel's father, retained him; and he abode with him three days: so they did eat and drink, and lodged there. Guidance: The verb 'played the whore against him' is ambiguous — Hebrew may also mean she was 'angry at him' or 'unfaithful to him.' The LXX reads 'was angry.' Either way, she leaves him and returns home for four months. The Levite goes after her 'to speak friendly' (literally 'to her heart') — he comes apologetically, not punitively. Her father is glad to host him; reconciliation seems possible. Three days of feasting. Watch how the chapter opens with the slow, ordinary domestic detail and then descends into the most brutal scene in scripture.

It came to pass on the fourth day, when they arose early in the morning, that he rose up to depart: and the damsel's father said unto his son in law, Comfort thine heart with a morsel of bread, and afterward go your way. They sat down, and did eat and drink both of them together: for the damsel's father had said unto the man, Be content, I pray thee, and tarry all night, and let thine heart be merry. When the man rose up to depart, his father in law urged him: therefore he lodged there again. He arose early in the morning on the fifth day to depart: and the damsel's father said, Comfort thine heart, I pray thee. And they tarried until afternoon, and they did eat both of them. When the man rose up to depart, he, and his concubine, and his servant, his father in law, the damsel's father, said unto him, Behold, now the day draweth toward evening, I pray you tarry all night: behold, the day groweth to an end, lodge here, that thine heart may be merry; and tomorrow get you early on your way, that thou mayest go home. Guidance: Five days of being detained by good food and bonhomie. The narrator slows the pace deliberately. The father-in-law's hospitality is excessive — almost obstructive. By delaying the departure into late afternoon, he forces them onto the road at dusk. Small kindnesses sometimes set up large tragedies. The chapter wants us to feel the chain of unhurried days that lead to a night that should have been avoided.

But the man would not tarry that night, but he rose up and departed, and came over against Jebus, which is Jerusalem; and there were with him two asses saddled, his concubine also was with him. When they were by Jebus, the day was far spent; and the servant said unto his master, Come, I pray thee, and let us turn in into this city of the Jebusites, and lodge in it. His master said unto him, We will not turn aside hither into the city of a stranger, that is not of the children of Israel; we will pass over to Gibeah. He said unto his servant, Come, and let us draw near to one of these places to lodge all night, in Gibeah, or in Ramah. Guidance: At dusk near Jebus (the future Jerusalem, still held by the Jebusites), the servant suggests stopping. The Levite refuses: 'we will not turn aside into the city of a stranger.' He prefers the Israelite town. The grim irony of the next paragraph: the foreign city would have been safer than the brother-tribe town. They press on to Gibeah of Benjamin. The man's confidence that his own people will be hospitable is about to be wrecked. Tribal pride is sometimes the most blinding kind.

They passed on and went their way; and the sun went down upon them when they were by Gibeah, which belongeth to Benjamin. They turned aside thither, to go in and to lodge in Gibeah: and when he went in, he sat him down in a street of the city: for there was no man that took them into his house to lodging. Guidance: A Levite, a servant, and a young woman sit in the town square at sunset and not one Benjamite offers them a roof. Hospitality in the ancient Near East was sacred — the law of nations. The town's silence is already a verdict. The chapter is going to disclose what kind of people would refuse a stranger this basic kindness. The empty hospitality is the canary in the coal mine of every other moral failure in town.

Behold, there came an old man from his work out of the field at even, which was also of mount Ephraim; and he sojourned in Gibeah: but the men of the place were Benjamites. When he had lifted up his eyes, he saw a wayfaring man in the street of the city: and the old man said, Whither goest thou? and whence comest thou? He said unto him, We are passing from Beth-lehem-judah toward the side of mount Ephraim; from thence am I: and I went to Beth-lehem-judah, but I am now going to the house of the LORD; and there is no man that receiveth me to house. Yet there is both straw and provender for our asses; and there is bread and wine also for me, and for thy handmaid, and for the young man which is with thy servants: there is no want of any thing. The old man said, Peace be with thee; howsoever let all thy wants lie upon me; only lodge not in the street. So he brought him into his house, and gave provender unto the asses: and they washed their feet, and did eat and drink. Guidance: The only host in Gibeah is also an outsider — an Ephraimite living among the Benjamites. The townspeople offered nothing; the resident alien offers everything. The old man's words deliberately echo Lot in Sodom (Gen 19): 'lodge not in the street.' He knows what the street becomes after dark in this town. The chapter is now openly playing the Sodom card. Gibeah is to Israel what Sodom was to the world — and the comparison is the worst indictment of an Israelite town that scripture can make.

As they were making their hearts merry, behold, the men of the city, certain sons of Belial, beset the house round about, and beat at the door, and spake to the master of the house, the old man, saying, Bring forth the man that came into thine house, that we may know him. The man, the master of the house, went out unto them, and said unto them, Nay, my brethren, nay, I pray you, do not so wickedly; seeing that this man is come into mine house, do not this folly. Behold, here is my daughter a maiden, and his concubine; them I will bring out now, and humble ye them, and do with them what seemeth good unto you: but unto this man do not so vile a thing. But the men would not hearken to him: so the man took his concubine, and brought her forth unto them; and they knew her, and abused her all the night until the morning: and when the day began to spring, they let her go. Then came the woman in the dawning of the day, and fell down at the door of the man's house where her lord was, till it was light. Guidance: The narrative deliberately rewrites Genesis 19 — every detail mirrors Sodom, except now it is Israelites doing the demanding. 'Sons of Belial' means 'worthless men.' The old man offers his own virgin daughter and the guest's concubine to spare the male traveler — every line in this verse is morally wrong, and the chapter does not pretend otherwise. The Levite himself pushes his concubine out the door to save his own skin. The narrator's spare description — 'they knew her, and abused her all the night' — is the only kind of restraint scripture has for what the Hebrew text otherwise spells out brutally. She is gang-raped through the night, dies at the threshold of the house where her husband sleeps. The chapter's gaze does not look away from her; her hands are on the threshold. There is no comment in the text. No verse defends her, condemns the cowardice, or rebukes the priesthood. The silence is the rebuke. The narrator is letting Israel see what it has become. The lack of a king is also the lack of justice; God's people have become Sodom on their own land.

The old man offers his own virgin daughter and the guest's concubine to spare the male traveler — every line in this verse is morally wrong, and the chapter does not pretend otherwise. The Levite himself pushes his concubine out the door to save his own skin. The narrator's spare description — 'they knew her, and abused her all the night' — is the only kind of restraint scripture has for what the Hebrew text otherwise spells out brutally. She is gang-raped through the night, dies at the threshold of the house where her husband sleeps. The chapter's gaze does not look away from her; her hands are on the threshold. There is no comment in the text. No verse defends her, condemns the cowardice, or rebukes the priesthood. The silence is the rebuke. The narrator is letting Israel see what it has become. The lack of a king is also the lack of justice; God's people have become Sodom on their own land.

As they were making their hearts merry, behold, the men of the city, certain sons of Belial, beset the house round about, and beat at the door, and spake to the master of the house, the old man, saying, Bring forth the man that came into thine house, that we may know him. The man, the master of the house, went out unto them, and said unto them, Nay, my brethren, nay, I pray you, do not so wickedly; seeing that this man is come into mine house, do not this folly. Behold, here is my daughter a maiden, and his concubine; them I will bring out now, and humble ye them, and do with them what seemeth good unto you: but unto this man do not so vile a thing. But the men would not hearken to him: so the man took his concubine, and brought her forth unto them; and they knew her, and abused her all the night until the morning: and when the day began to spring, they let her go. Then came the woman in the dawning of the day, and fell down at the door of the man's house where her lord was, till it was light. Guidance: The narrative deliberately rewrites Genesis 19 — every detail mirrors Sodom, except now it is Israelites doing the demanding. 'Sons of Belial' means 'worthless men.' The old man offers his own virgin daughter and the guest's concubine to spare the male traveler — every line in this verse is morally wrong, and the chapter does not pretend otherwise. The Levite himself pushes his concubine out the door to save his own skin. The narrator's spare description — 'they knew her, and abused her all the night' — is the only kind of restraint scripture has for what the Hebrew text otherwise spells out brutally. She is gang-raped through the night, dies at the threshold of the house where her husband sleeps. The chapter's gaze does not look away from her; her hands are on the threshold. There is no comment in the text. No verse defends her, condemns the cowardice, or rebukes the priesthood. The silence is the rebuke. The narrator is letting Israel see what it has become. The lack of a king is also the lack of justice; God's people have become Sodom on their own land.

The man, the master of the house, went out unto them, and said unto them, Nay, my brethren, nay, I pray you, do not so wickedly; seeing that this man is come into mine house, do not this folly. Behold, here is my daughter a maiden, and his concubine; them I will bring out now, and humble ye them, and do with them what seemeth good unto you: but unto this man do not so vile a thing. Her lord rose up in the morning, and opened the doors of the house, and went out to go his way: and, behold, the woman his concubine was fallen down at the door of the house, and her hands were upon the threshold. He said unto her, Up, and let us be going. But none answered. Then the man took her up upon an ass, and the man rose up, and gat him unto his place. Guidance: The chapter's most chilling line is one of casual dismissal. The Levite steps over his own murdered wife to head home, then notices she is there, and his first words are 'Up, let us be going.' No grief, no horror, no name spoken. Then 'none answered.' She is dead. He throws her body on a donkey like luggage. His coldness is a deeper indictment than the rapists' rage. The chapter has spent its emotional weight on her hands on the threshold — a detail the narrator forces every reader to see. Even when the world will not name a woman, scripture holds her hands on the threshold so we cannot.

Her lord rose up in the morning, and opened the doors of the house, and went out to go his way: and, behold, the woman his concubine was fallen down at the door of the house, and her hands were upon the threshold. He said unto her, Up, and let us be going. But none answered. Then the man took her up upon an ass, and the man rose up, and gat him unto his place. Guidance: The chapter's most chilling line is one of casual dismissal. The Levite steps over his own murdered wife to head home, then notices she is there, and his first words are 'Up, let us be going.' No grief, no horror, no name spoken. Then 'none answered.' She is dead. He throws her body on a donkey like luggage. His coldness is a deeper indictment than the rapists' rage. The chapter has spent its emotional weight on her hands on the threshold — a detail the narrator forces every reader to see. Even when the world will not name a woman, scripture holds her hands on the threshold so we cannot.

When he was come into his house, he took a knife, and laid hold on his concubine, and divided her, together with her bones, into twelve pieces, and sent her into all the coasts of Israel. It was so, that all that saw it said, There was no such deed done nor seen from the day that the children of Israel came up out of the land of Egypt unto this day: consider of it, take advice, and speak your minds. Guidance: He butchers her body into twelve pieces and sends them, one to each tribe, as a summons to gather. A grotesque parody of the Levitical sacrifice (which divided animals into pieces) — and a horrific way to publicize a crime. The man too cowardly to defend her now uses her remains as political signal-fire. Yet the call works: Israel reacts in unprecedented horror. 'There was no such deed done nor seen from the day that the children of Israel came up out of Egypt unto this day' — the worst thing since the exodus. The chapter ends with the entire nation looking at pieces of one woman and finally asking themselves what they have become. The next two chapters are Israel's answer, and it is its own catastrophe. Sin's true depth often becomes visible only when the most vulnerable are destroyed; God's people sometimes need to look at the body before they will look at the sin.
